perturbation.original_text: a) in the event of a material breach or default by the other Party of a material obligation of such Party under the Agreement which is not remedied within [*] Days after a written notice is given of such default or breach;
perturbation.changed_text: a) in the event of a material breach or default by the other Party of a material obligation of such Party under the Agreement which is not remedied within five (5) Days after a written notice is given of such default or breach;
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period for a material breach to 5 days may be unreasonably short, especially for complex breaches. This is a change because parties are usually afforded reasonable time to cure breaches.
perturbation.law_explanation: California law generally implies a reasonable time for performance when a contract does not specify a deadline. A 5-day cure period for a material breach might be deemed unreasonably short, conflicting with the implied reasonable time for performance.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1670 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1670. Any dispute arising from a construction contract with a public agency, which contract contains a provision that one party to the contract or one party’s agent or employee shall decide any disputes arising under that contract, shall be resolved by submitting the dispute to independent arbitration, if mutually agreeable, otherwise by litigation in a court of competent jurisdiction. (Added by Stats. 1978, Ch. 1374.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract allows for a cure period of [*] days for a material breach. The changed text reduces this to a fixed 5 days. The Law Explanation states that California law implies a reasonable time for performance when a contract does not specify a deadline. A 5-day cure period may be deemed unreasonably short, which contradicts the implied 'reasonable time' for performance, making the contract potentially unenforceable. Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not directly address cure periods or reasonable time, but it does indicate that California law governs contracts. The change introduces a specific, potentially unreasonable, timeframe that could be challenged in court as not providing a 'reasonable time' to cure, especially given the complexity often involved in material breaches, creating a legal inconsistency and therefore, a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Any legal or other action related to a breach of this Agreement must be commenced no later than [*] years from the date of the breach in a court sited within the State of New York.
perturbation.changed_text: Any legal or other action related to a breach of this Agreement must be commenced no later than one (1) year from the date of the breach in a court sited within the State of New York.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the statute of limitations to one year could be problematic as it may be shorter than what is legally allowed for contract breaches in New York.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York CPLR § 213 prescribes a six-year statute of limitations for breach of contract actions. Reducing it to one year is a violation of New York state law.

Civil Practice Law & Rules (CVP) CHAPTER 8, ARTICLE 2 § 213. Actions to be commenced within six years: where not otherwise provided for; on contract; on sealed instrument; on bond or note, and mortgage upon real property; by state based on misappropriation of public property;

Laws (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 New York Laws CVP - Civil Practice Law and Rules Article 2 - Limitations of Time 213 - Actions to Be Commenced Within Six Years: Where Not Otherwise Provided For; on Contract; on Sealed Instrument; on Bond or Note, and Mortgage Upon Real Universal Citation: NY CPLR § 213 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 213. Actions to be commenced within six years: where not otherwise provided for; on contract; on sealed instrument; on bond or note, and mortgage upon real property; by state based on misappropriation of public property; based on mistake; by corporation against director, officer or stockholder; based on fraud. The following actions must be commenced within six years: 1. an action for which no limitation is specifically prescribed by law; 2. an action upon a contractual obligation or liability, express or implied, except as provided in section two hundred thirteen-a or two hundred fourteen-i of this article or article 2 of the uniform commercial code or article 36-B of the general business law; 3. an action upon a sealed instrument; 4. an action upon a bond or note, the payment of which is secured by a mortgage upon real property, or upon a bond or note and mortgage so secured, or upon a mortgage of real property, or any interest therein; (a) In any action on an instrument described under this subdivision, if the statute of limitations is raised as a defense, and if that defense is based on a claim that the instrument at issue was accelerated
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the statute of limitations for breach of contract to one year, while New York CPLR § 213 explicitly states a six-year statute of limitations for such actions. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2 both confirm this six-year period. This direct conflict constitutes a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Attachment will automatically be renewed for periods of six (6) months unless either party gives twelve (12) months written notice of its intent to terminate this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Attachment will automatically be renewed for periods of six (6) months unless either party gives one (1) month written notice of its intent to terminate this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring only a 1 month notice to not renew when the automatic renewal is for 6 months could be seen as unconscionable in court and not commercially reasonable.
perturbation.law_explanation: While this contract is between two businesses, implied in every contract is good faith. A 1 month period for a six month renewal could be seen as not acting in good faith if the company not being properly notified is materially harmed.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.7. CONSUMER WARRANTIES [1790 - 1797.96] ( Title 1.7 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1333. ) CHAPTER 1. Consumer Warranty Protection [1790 - 1795.8] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1333. ) ARTICLE 3. Sale Warranties [1792 - 1795.8] ( Article 3 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1333. ) 1793.02. (a) (1) Except as provided in paragraph (2), all new and used assistive devices sold at retail in this state shall be accompanied by the retail seller’s written warranty which shall contain the following language: “This assistive device is warranted to be specifically fit for the particular needs of you, the buyer. If the device is not specifically fit for your particular needs, it may be returned to the seller within 30 days of the date of actual receipt by you or completion of fitting by the seller, whichever occurs later. If you return the device, the seller will either adjust or replace the device or promptly refund the total amount paid. This warranty does not affect the protections and remedies you have under other laws.” In lieu of the words “30 days
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the notification period for terminating the agreement from twelve (12) months to one (1) month. The perturbation explanation indicates this change could be seen as unconscionable and not commercially reasonable. The Law Explanation highlights that the contract has implied good faith, and a one-month notification period for a six-month renewal may not be in good faith if one party is materially harmed. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, where the time-sensitive obligation (notice period) does not align with what is commercially reasonable, potentially violating good faith and making the terms unenforceable. Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses consumer warranties but is less directly applicable than the good faith argument made in the Law Explanation, supporting a potential legal contradiction.
